questions.question: Explain the difference between 0/1 Knapsack and Fractional Knapsack problems.
keyPoints: 0/1: items can be taken wholly or not at all; Fractional: items can be taken partially
0/1 requires Dynamic Programming; Fractional can be solved by Greedy method
0/1 has no greedy choice property; Fractional has greedy choice property

questions.question: Define optimal substructure and overlapping subproblems with reference to Knapsack Problem.
keyPoints: Optimal substructure: optimal solution uses optimal solutions of subproblems
Overlapping subproblems: same subproblems computed multiple times
In Knapsack: K[n][W] depends on K[n-1][W] and K[n-1][w-wt[n]]
Many weight-item combinations repeat during computation

questions.question: What is memoization? How does it improve the recursive solution for Knapsack?
keyPoints: Memoization stores computed results in a table for future lookup
Avoids recomputation of same subproblems
Converts exponential time to pseudo-polynomial O(n×W)
Also called top-down DP approach

questions.question: Explain why Greedy approach fails for 0/1 Knapsack but works for Fractional Knapsack.
keyPoints: Greedy fails for 0/1 because it lacks greedy choice property
Local optimal (high ratio item) may not lead to global optimum
Fractional works because taking best ratio item first never hurts optimality
In 0/1, taking high ratio item may exclude better combination

questions.question: How can space complexity of 0/1 Knapsack be optimized from O(nW) to O(W)?
keyPoints: Use 1D array instead of 2D DP table
Iterate capacity from W down to weight[i]
Processing right-to-left prevents using same item twice
Only stores current row instead of entire table

questions.question: What is a Memory Function? Describe its working for the Knapsack Problem.
keyPoints: Memory function combines recursion with memoization
Initializes lookup table with sentinel value (-1)
When called, checks if subproblem already solved before computing
Solves only required subproblems (top-down with caching)

questions.question: Write the recurrence relation for 0/1 Knapsack and explain each term.
keyPoints: K[i-1][w]: exclude current item, value from previous items
K[i-1][w-wt[i-1]] + val[i-1]: include current item if weight permits
Base cases: K[0][w] = 0, K[i][0] = 0

questions.question: Solve the following 0/1 Knapsack problem using Dynamic Programming: n=4, weights=[2,3,4,5], values=[3,4,5,6], capacity W=5. Show the complete DP table and trace the solution.
keyPoints: Create DP table of size 5×6
Fill base cases (row 0 and column 0 = 0)
Compute each cell using recurrence
Find optimal value = 7 (items with weight 2 and 3)
Trace back to find selected items

questions.question: Explain the Dynamic Programming approach for 0/1 Knapsack. Write the algorithm and analyze its time and space complexity.
keyPoints: Explain optimal substructure and overlapping subproblems
Write pseudo-code with nested loops
Time complexity: O(n×W) - pseudo-polynomial
Space complexity: O(n×W) or O(W) with optimization
Discuss memoization vs tabulation approaches

questions.question: Compare Top-Down (Memoization) and Bottom-Up (Tabulation) approaches for Knapsack. When would you prefer one over the other?
keyPoints: Top-down: recursive with memoization, solves only needed subproblems
Bottom-up: iterative table filling, solves all subproblems
Top-down: less computations if few subproblems needed
Bottom-up: no recursion overhead, better cache behavior
Choice depends on problem structure and requirement

questions.question: Solve Fractional Knapsack for: weights=[10,20,30], values=[60,100,120], capacity=50. Show step-by-step greedy solution.
keyPoints: Calculate value/weight ratios: 6, 5, 4
Sort by ratio descending: ratio 6 (item1), 5 (item2), 4 (item3)
Take item1 fully: weight 10, value 60, remaining capacity 40
Take item2 fully: weight 20, value 100, remaining capacity 20
Take fraction of item3: weight 20, value 80 (120×20/30)
Total value = 60+100+80=240
